#### Phase 8: Submission — TestFlight, App Store Metadata, Review Prep

**Purpose:** Get the app through Apple's review and into users' hands. This is a separate phase because submission is not trivial — it requires specific artifacts, compliance checks, and a review strategy.

**Entry Criteria:**
- Phase 7 exit criteria fully met
- App is feature-complete and passes all tests
- Legal has reviewed privacy policy and terms of service
- Marketing has approved app name, description, and screenshots

**Task List:**

| # | Task | Depends On | Est. Hours |
|---|------|-----------|-----------|
| 8.1 | Create App Store Connect listing: app name, subtitle, category (Business), content rating, pricing (free), availability (US initially or global) | None | 2 |
| 8.2 | Prepare app metadata: description (4000 char max, keyword-optimized), keywords (100 char max, comma-separated), promotional text (170 char max), support URL, privacy policy URL, marketing URL | 8.1 | 3 |
| 8.3 | Capture App Store screenshots: required sizes (6.7", 6.5", 5.5" — at minimum 6.7" and 5.5"); capture actual app screens (not mockups); include: chat, people, property detail, studio, camera; dark and light variants if desired; add marketing frames/titles if desired | 8.1 | 6 |
| 8.4 | Build production binary: `xcodebuild -scheme Skye -configuration Release -archivePath build/Skye.xcarchive archive` followed by `xcodebuild -exportArchive -archivePath build/Skye.xcarchive -exportOptionsPlist ExportOptions.plist -exportPath build/` (or use Xcode Organizer: Product > Archive); verify bundle identifier, version (`CFBundleShortVersionString`), build number (`CFBundleVersion`); verify app icon and launch screen are correct in the built IPA. Alternatively, use fastlane `gym` or Xcode Cloud for automated archive + export | None | 2 |
| 8.5 | Deploy to TestFlight: upload production archive to App Store Connect via Xcode Organizer "Distribute App" > "TestFlight & App Store", or `xcrun altool --upload-app`, or `xcrun notarytool` + App Store Connect API; add internal testers; verify app installs and runs on TestFlight; run full Maestro / XCUITest suite against TestFlight build | 8.4 | 3 |
| 8.6 | Internal beta testing: 5-10 testers use the app for 3-7 days; collect feedback via structured form (bugs, UX issues, performance complaints); triage and fix critical issues; rebuild and re-deploy if needed | 8.5 | 16 |
| 8.7 | Prepare App Review notes: explain features that require special configuration (e.g., "To test push notifications, send a test push from our admin panel"); provide demo credentials if login requires invitation; explain any features that may seem incomplete but are intentional | 8.6 | 2 |
| 8.8 | Compliance checklist before submission: (1) Account deletion works, (2) Privacy manifest complete, (3) Privacy nutrition labels accurate, (4) No IDFA usage (or ATT prompt via `AppTrackingTransparency` if used), (5) No third-party payment for digital goods, (6) Export compliance answered correctly (`ITSAppUsesNonExemptEncryption` set in `Info.plist`), (7) All permission purpose strings (`NSCameraUsageDescription`, `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription`, etc.) are specific and clear, (8) App does not crash on any screen, (9) All links work (privacy policy, support URL), (10) Content moderation is in place for any UGC | 8.6 | 3 |
| 8.9 | Submit for App Review: submit via App Store Connect; select "Manual release" (not auto-release) to control launch timing; monitor review status | 8.7, 8.8 | 1 |
| 8.10 | Handle review feedback: if rejected, analyze rejection reason, fix the specific issue, re-submit with detailed notes explaining the fix; common native Swift rejections: crash on specific device (test on that device), permission usage description too vague (rewrite), HIG violation (adjust UI) | 8.9 | 8 (estimate) |
| 8.11 | Release: once approved, trigger release; monitor Sentry for crash-free rate; monitor App Store Connect for user reviews | 8.10 | 1 |

**Exit Criteria:**
- App is live on the App Store
- Sentry crash-free rate is >99.5% in first 48 hours
- No 1-star reviews citing bugs in first week
- TestFlight remains available for continued internal testing

**Decision Gate:**
After TestFlight beta, decide if app quality is sufficient for public release or if an additional polish sprint is needed. This is the last off-ramp before the app is public.

**Deliverables:**
- App Store listing with metadata and screenshots
- Production binary on TestFlight
- Beta test report with resolutions
- App Review submission
- Live app on App Store

**Verification Checklist:**
- [ ] TestFlight build installs and runs on 3+ different iPhone models
- [ ] 5+ internal testers have used the app for 3+ days
- [ ] All critical beta feedback is resolved
- [ ] Compliance checklist has 10/10 checks passing
- [ ] App Review submission is accepted (not rejected)
- [ ] App is live and downloadable from App Store
- [ ] Sentry shows >99.5% crash-free sessions

---

### 2. Dependency Graph

#### Phase-Level Dependencies

```
Phase 0 ────────→ Phase 1 ────────→ Phase 2 ────────→ Phase 4 ──┐
                     │                                             │
                     │                  Phase 3 ──────────────────→├──→ Phase 7 ──→ Phase 8
                     │                    ↑                        │
                     └────────────────────┘                        │
                     │                                             │
                     └──────────→ Phase 5 ────────────────────────→┘
                     │                                             │
                     └──────────→ Phase 6 ────────────────────────→┘
```

**Detailed dependency rules:**

| Phase | Hard Dependencies | Can Start When | Can Overlap With |
|-------|------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 0 | None | Immediately | Nothing (must complete first) |
| 1 | Phase 0 complete | Phase 0 exits | Nothing (critical path) |
| 2 | Phase 1 complete | Phase 1 exits | Phase 3 (different developer) |
| 3 | Phase 1 complete | Phase 1 exits | Phase 2 (different developer), Phase 5 partially |
| 4 | Phase 2 + Phase 3 complete | Phase 3 exits (needs contact list + chat tool cards) | Phase 5, Phase 6 |
| 5 | Phase 1 complete (auth + nav); Phase 2 + 3 recommended (screens to navigate to) | Phase 3 exits | Phase 4, Phase 6 |
| 6 | Phase 1 complete (foundation); Phase 2 recommended (SSE infrastructure reuse) | Phase 2 exits | Phase 4, Phase 5 |
| 7 | Phases 1-6 all complete | Phase 6 exits (last feature phase) | Nothing (full-app audit) |
| 8 | Phase 7 complete | Phase 7 exits | Nothing (submission requires stability) |

#### Critical Path (Minimum Calendar Time)

```
Phase 0 → Phase 1 → Phase 2 → Phase 4 → Phase 7 → Phase 8
  (2w)      (3w)      (3w)      (2.5w)    (2w)      (2w)
                                                    = ~14.5 weeks
```

With parallelization (2 developers):

```
                               Developer A              Developer B
Phase 0 (2 weeks)             [Both developers]
Phase 1 (3 weeks)             [Both developers]
Phase 2 (3 weeks)             [Chat screen]             [People tab - Phase 3]
Phase 4 (2.5 weeks)           [Detail screens]          [Push & Deep Links - Phase 5]
Phase 6 (2 weeks)             [Camera & Studio]          ←── (joins Phase 6)
Phase 7 (2 weeks)             [Both developers]
Phase 8 (2 weeks)             [Both developers]
                                                        = ~11.5 weeks with 2 devs
```

#### Task-Level Parallelization Within Phases

**Phase 1** (with 2 developers):
- Dev A: Tasks 1.1-1.2-1.10-1.14 (Xcode project scaffold, navigation, auth, splash)
- Dev B: Tasks 1.3-1.4-1.5-1.6-1.7-1.8 (theming, storage layers, state management)
- Converge: Tasks 1.9 (API client needs auth state), 1.11-1.16 (shared utilities)

**Phase 2** (mostly sequential due to tight coupling):
- Parallel: 2.1/2.8 (screen shell + input bar) can be built simultaneously
- Parallel: 2.6 (markdown) and 2.7 (tool cards) are independent after 2.4
- Sequential: 2.5 (SSE) must precede 2.13 (error recovery); 2.9 (persistence) must precede 2.12 (memory management)

**Phase 4** (with 2 developers):
- Dev A: Tasks 4.1-4.4 (contact detail)
- Dev B: Tasks 4.6-4.9 (properties)
- Converge: Tasks 4.5, 4.10 (wiring tool cards)

#### Backend Change Timeline

| When | Endpoint | Change Type |
|------|----------|------------|
| Phase 0 | `/api/health` | New |
| Phase 0 | `/api/auth/native` | New |
| Phase 0 | `/api/user/delete` | New |
| Phase 0 | All 129 routes | Audit (JSON responses, CORS) |
| Phase 5 | `/api/push/subscribe` | Modify |
| Phase 5 | `/api/push/send` | Modify |

---

### 3. Architecture Decision Records — Final Recommendations

---

#### ADR-1: Auth Library

**Decision:** `ASWebAuthenticationSession` with PKCE flow

**Justification:**
- `ASWebAuthenticationSession` is Apple's first-party API for OAuth authentication flows on iOS. It provides a secure, system-managed browser session that shares cookies with Safari, supports PKCE natively, and requires no third-party dependencies
- It works out of the box with any OAuth 2.0 provider including Google, with no SDK installation or native module configuration needed
- The alternative, Google Sign-In SDK for iOS (`GoogleSignIn`), provides a more branded UI but introduces a heavyweight dependency, requires `GIDConfiguration` setup, and adds a transitive dependency on `GTMSessionFetcher`. Since Skye only needs the auth code (not ongoing Google API access), the full Google SDK is unnecessary
- Supabase integration is straightforward: the auth code from Google is sent to our own `/api/auth/native` backend endpoint, which performs the server-side token exchange (keeping the Google client secret on the server) and returns Supabase-compatible tokens

**Implementation Sketch:**

```swift
import AuthenticationServices

@Observable
final class AuthService {
    private let apiClient: APIClient
    private let keychainManager: KeychainManager

    func signInWithGoogle() async throws -> User {
        let codeVerifier = generateCodeVerifier()
        let codeChallenge = generateCodeChallenge(from: codeVerifier)

        let authURL = buildGoogleAuthURL(
            clientId: Configuration.googleIOSClientId,
            redirectURI: "com.googleusercontent.apps.\(Configuration.googleIOSClientId):/oauthredirect",
            codeChallenge: codeChallenge,
            scopes: ["openid", "profile", "email"]
        )

        // Present ASWebAuthenticationSession
        let callbackURL = try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation { continuation in
            let session = ASWebAuthenticationSession(
                url: authURL,
                callbackURLScheme: "com.googleusercontent.apps.\(Configuration.googleIOSClientId)"
            ) { callbackURL, error in
                if let error { continuation.resume(throwing: error) }
                else if let callbackURL { continuation.resume(returning: callbackURL) }
            }
            session.presentationContextProvider = self
            session.prefersEphemeralWebBrowserSession = false
            session.start()
        }

        // Extract auth code from callback URL
        let code = try extractAuthCode(from: callbackURL)

        // Send to our backend — NOT directly to Google token endpoint
        let response: AuthResponse = try await apiClient.post("/api/auth/native", body: [
            "code": code,
            "codeVerifier": codeVerifier,
            "redirectUri": "com.googleusercontent.apps.\(Configuration.googleIOSClientId):/oauthredirect"
        ])

        // Store tokens
        try keychainManager.store(response.refreshToken, for: .refreshToken)
        AuthState.shared.login(
            accessToken: response.accessToken,
            user: response.user
        )

        return response.user
    }

    private func generateCodeVerifier() -> String {
        var bytes = [UInt8](repeating: 0, count: 32)
        _ = SecRandomCopyBytes(kSecRandomDefault, bytes.count, &bytes)
        return Data(bytes).base64EncodedString()
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "+", with: "-")
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "/", with: "_")
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "=", with: "")
    }

    private func generateCodeChallenge(from verifier: String) -> String {
        let data = Data(verifier.utf8)
        var hash = [UInt8](repeating: 0, count: 32)
        data.withUnsafeBytes { _ = CC_SHA256($0.baseAddress, CC_LONG(data.count), &hash) }
        return Data(hash).base64EncodedString()
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "+", with: "-")
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "/", with: "_")
            .replacingOccurrences(of: "=", with: "")
    }
}
```

**Redirect URI Format:** `com.googleusercontent.apps.{IOS_CLIENT_ID}:/oauthredirect`

**Token Storage:** Access token in `AuthState` `@Observable` class (memory) + `UserDefaults` (for persistence across restarts). Refresh token in Keychain via `KeychainManager` (Security framework). Refresh token is NEVER stored in UserDefaults or any unencrypted storage.

---

#### ADR-2: SSE/Streaming

**Decision:** Native `URLSession` bytes streaming API

**Justification:**
- Swift's `URLSession` provides first-class support for streaming responses via `URLSession.AsyncBytes` (available since iOS 15). This eliminates the need for any third-party SSE library
- The native implementation compiles directly to machine code via LLVM — there is no JavaScript bridge, no polyfill layer, and no runtime overhead. Streaming tokens arrive and are processed with minimal latency
- `URLSession` handles TLS, HTTP/2, connection pooling, and background task management natively through the operating system's networking stack
- The alternative of using a third-party SSE library (e.g., `LDSwiftEventSource` from LaunchDarkly) adds a dependency for functionality that Swift's standard library already provides. The only advantage would be automatic `Last-Event-ID` handling, which can be implemented in ~10 lines of custom code

**Implementation Sketch:**

```swift
actor StreamingChatService {
    private let apiClient: APIClient
    private var activeTask: Task<Void, Never>?

    enum StreamState: Sendable {
        case idle, connecting, streaming, error(Error)
    }

    func sendMessage(
        _ content: String,
        conversationId: String,
        onDelta: @escaping @Sendable (String) -> Void,
        onToolUse: @escaping @Sendable (ToolCall) -> Void,
        onComplete: @escaping @Sendable () -> Void,
        onError: @escaping @Sendable (Error) -> Void
    ) {
        activeTask?.cancel()

        activeTask = Task {
            do {
                let url = URL(string: "\(Configuration.apiBase)/api/chat/stream")!
                var components = URLComponents(url: url, resolvingAgainstBaseURL: false)!
                components.queryItems = [
                    URLQueryItem(name: "conversationId", value: conversationId),
                    URLQueryItem(name: "message", value: content)
                ]

                var request = URLRequest(url: components.url!)
                request.setValue(
                    "Bearer \(AuthState.shared.accessToken)",
                    forHTTPHeaderField: "Authorization"
                )
                request.setValue("text/event-stream", forHTTPHeaderField: "Accept")

                let (bytes, response) = try await URLSession.shared.bytes(for: request)

                guard let httpResponse = response as? HTTPURLResponse,
                      httpResponse.statusCode == 200 else {
                    throw StreamError.invalidResponse
                }

                for try await line in bytes.lines {
                    guard !Task.isCancelled else { break }

                    guard line.hasPrefix("data: ") else { continue }
                    let jsonString = String(line.dropFirst(6))
                    guard jsonString != "[DONE]" else {
                        onComplete()
                        break
                    }

                    let event = try JSONDecoder().decode(StreamEvent.self, from: Data(jsonString.utf8))
                    switch event.type {
                    case .contentDelta:
                        onDelta(event.text ?? "")
                    case .toolUse:
                        if let toolCall = event.toolCall {
                            onToolUse(toolCall)
                        }
                    case .contentStop:
                        onComplete()
                    case .error:
                        onError(StreamError.serverError(event.message ?? "Unknown"))
                    }
                }
            } catch {
                if !Task.isCancelled {
                    onError(error)
                }
            }
        }
    }

    func cancel() {
        activeTask?.cancel()
        activeTask = nil
    }
}
```

**Reconnection Strategy:**
- On disconnect: exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, max 30s) using `Task.sleep(nanoseconds:)`
- After 3 consecutive failures: stop auto-reconnect, show manual retry button
- On reconnect success: if mid-message, re-request from the last received event ID (if backend supports `Last-Event-ID`) or restart the message
- Partial response preservation: store streamed tokens in an `@Observable` buffer; on disconnect, the buffer content is displayed as-is with a "Connection lost" indicator

---

#### ADR-3: Offline Storage

**Decision:** Dual-layer — `GRDB.swift` for structured data + `UserDefaults` / `@AppStorage` for key-value data

**Justification:**
- **GRDB.swift** (via SPM) is the right choice for contacts (500+), properties, conversations, and messages because this data has relational structure (contacts have activities, properties have images, conversations have messages) and requires complex queries (search, filter, sort, paginate). GRDB provides a full-featured Swift wrapper around SQLite with type-safe query building, `Codable` record mapping, and reactive observation via `ValueObservation`. It is the most widely adopted SQLite library in the Swift ecosystem
- **UserDefaults / @AppStorage** is the right choice for fast key-value access: theme preference, onboarding state, last sync timestamps, feature flags. `@AppStorage` integrates directly with SwiftUI for reactive updates — when a stored value changes, views automatically re-render. For non-SwiftUI contexts, `UserDefaults` provides synchronous access with negligible overhead
- **Keychain Services** (Security framework) is used exclusively for refresh tokens — this is not general-purpose storage, it is a security-specific choice. The iOS Keychain encrypts data at rest and survives app reinstalls (optionally)
- The alternative of using only `SwiftData` for everything was considered but rejected: SwiftData requires iOS 17+ minimum deployment target (too restrictive) and has known issues with complex migration scenarios as of 2025-2026. GRDB provides more control over the SQLite layer and supports iOS 15+

**Schema Overview (GRDB / SQLite):**

```swift
// GRDB Record types with Codable conformance

struct Contact: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord, Identifiable {
    var id: String
    var firstName: String
    var lastName: String
    var email: String?
    var phone: String?
    var company: String?
    var role: String?
    var leadStatus: String = "lead"
    var tags: [String]? // Encoded as JSON
    var address: Address? // Encoded as JSON
    var notes: String?
    var source: String?
    var createdAt: Date
    var updatedAt: Date
    var syncedAt: Date?

    static let databaseTableName = "contacts"
}

struct Property: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord, Identifiable {
    var id: String
    var address: String
    var city: String?
    var state: String?
    var zip: String?
    var price: Double?
    var beds: Int?
    var baths: Double?
    var sqft: Int?
    var lotSize: Double?
    var yearBuilt: Int?
    var mlsNumber: String?
    var status: String = "active"
    var description: String?
    var images: [String]? // JSON array of URLs
    var listingAgentId: String?
    var createdAt: Date
    var updatedAt: Date
    var syncedAt: Date?

    static let databaseTableName = "properties"
}

struct Conversation: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord, Identifiable {
    var id: String
    var title: String?
    var lastMessagePreview: String?
    var lastMessageAt: Date?
    var messageCount: Int = 0
    var createdAt: Date
    var updatedAt: Date
    var syncedAt: Date?

    static let databaseTableName = "conversations"
}

struct Message: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord, Identifiable {
    var id: String
    var conversationId: String
    var role: String // "user" | "assistant"
    var content: String
    var toolCalls: [ToolCall]? // JSON array of tool invocations
    var createdAt: Date

    static let databaseTableName = "messages"
}

struct StudioDraft: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord, Identifiable {
    var id: String
    var templateType: String
    var inputs: String // JSON
    var output: String
    var createdAt: Date

    static let databaseTableName = "studio_drafts"
}

struct SyncMeta: Codable, FetchableRecord, PersistableRecord {
    var entityType: String // "contacts" | "properties" | "conversations"
    var lastSyncedAt: Date
    var lastCursor: String?

    static let databaseTableName = "sync_meta"
}

// Database migration setup
var migrator = DatabaseMigrator()

migrator.registerMigration("001_initial") { db in
    try db.create(table: "contacts") { t in
        t.primaryKey("id", .text)
        t.column("firstName", .text).notNull()
        t.column("lastName", .text).notNull()
        t.column("email", .text)
        t.column("phone", .text)
        t.column("company", .text)
        t.column("role", .text)
        t.column("leadStatus", .text).defaults(to: "lead")
        t.column("tags", .text) // JSON array
        t.column("address", .text) // JSON object
        t.column("notes", .text)
        t.column("source", .text)
        t.column("createdAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("updatedAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("syncedAt", .text)
    }

    try db.create(table: "properties") { t in
        t.primaryKey("id", .text)
        t.column("address", .text).notNull()
        t.column("city", .text)
        t.column("state", .text)
        t.column("zip", .text)
        t.column("price", .double)
        t.column("beds", .integer)
        t.column("baths", .double)
        t.column("sqft", .integer)
        t.column("lotSize", .double)
        t.column("yearBuilt", .integer)
        t.column("mlsNumber", .text)
        t.column("status", .text).defaults(to: "active")
        t.column("description", .text)
        t.column("images", .text) // JSON array of URLs
        t.column("listingAgentId", .text)
        t.column("createdAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("updatedAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("syncedAt", .text)
    }

    try db.create(table: "conversations") { t in
        t.primaryKey("id", .text)
        t.column("title", .text)
        t.column("lastMessagePreview", .text)
        t.column("lastMessageAt", .text)
        t.column("messageCount", .integer).defaults(to: 0)
        t.column("createdAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("updatedAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("syncedAt", .text)
    }

    try db.create(table: "messages") { t in
        t.primaryKey("id", .text)
        t.column("conversationId", .text).notNull()
            .references("conversations", onDelete: .cascade)
        t.column("role", .text).notNull()
        t.column("content", .text).notNull()
        t.column("toolCalls", .text) // JSON array
        t.column("createdAt", .text).notNull()
    }

    try db.create(table: "studio_drafts") { t in
        t.primaryKey("id", .text)
        t.column("templateType", .text).notNull()
        t.column("inputs", .text).notNull() // JSON
        t.column("output", .text).notNull()
        t.column("createdAt", .text).notNull()
    }

    try db.create(table: "sync_meta") { t in
        t.primaryKey("entityType", .text)
        t.column("lastSyncedAt", .text).notNull()
        t.column("lastCursor", .text)
    }

    // Indexes for search performance
    try db.create(index: "idx_contacts_name", on: "contacts", columns: ["firstName", "lastName"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_contacts_email", on: "contacts", columns: ["email"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_contacts_phone", on: "contacts", columns: ["phone"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_contacts_lead_status", on: "contacts", columns: ["leadStatus"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_properties_status", on: "properties", columns: ["status"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_properties_price", on: "properties", columns: ["price"])
    try db.create(index: "idx_messages_conversation", on: "messages", columns: ["conversationId", "createdAt"])
}
```

**Migration System:**
- `sync_meta` tracks schema version
- Migrations are registered sequentially with `DatabaseMigrator` (`001_initial`, `002_add_fts`, etc.)
- On app launch, `AppDatabase.swift` calls `migrator.migrate(database)` which checks current version and runs any pending migrations
- Migrations are irreversible (no down migrations — not needed for mobile)

---

#### ADR-4: State Management

**Decision:** `@Observable` classes (client state) + structured async/await data layer (server state) + `UserDefaults` / `@AppStorage` (persistence)

**Justification:**
- Swift's `@Observable` macro (Observation framework, iOS 17+) provides automatic, fine-grained UI updates without the boilerplate of `@Published` + `ObservableObject`. When a view reads a property of an `@Observable` class, SwiftUI automatically tracks that dependency and re-renders only when that specific property changes. This replaces the need for both Zustand (client state) and TanStack Query (server state caching) from the React Native world
- **Server data** (contacts, properties, conversations, studio output) is managed through `Repository` classes that are `@Observable`. Each repository handles fetching, caching, pagination, retry, and offline fallback to GRDB. GRDB's `ValueObservation` provides reactive database observation — when data changes in SQLite, the UI updates automatically
- **Client-only state** (auth state, UI state, network state, streaming state) is managed through simple `@Observable` singleton classes. Since Swift's `@Observable` is built into the language, there is no need for a third-party state management library
- **Persistence** uses `@AppStorage` for simple values (theme, onboarding flags) and GRDB for complex data. This is simpler than the React Native equivalent because `@AppStorage` is a SwiftUI property wrapper that reads/writes `UserDefaults` and triggers view updates automatically — no custom persistence middleware needed
- The separation is clean: if it comes from the server, it lives in a `Repository`. If it is generated client-side and never sent to a server, it lives in a state `@Observable` class

**State Separation:**

```
State/
  AuthState.swift           — user, tokens, isAuthenticated, login(), logout()
  UIState.swift             — theme, activeSheet, toastQueue
  NetworkState.swift        — isConnected, lastConnectedAt
  StreamState.swift         — activeStreamId, streamState, partialContent

Repositories/
  ContactsRepository.swift  — fetch, cache, paginate /api/contacts
  ContactRepository.swift   — fetch single /api/contacts/:id
  PropertiesRepository.swift — fetch, cache, paginate /api/properties
  PropertyRepository.swift  — fetch single /api/properties/:id
  ConversationsRepository.swift — fetch /api/conversations
  MessagesRepository.swift  — fetch, paginate /api/conversations/:id/messages
  ActivityRepository.swift  — fetch, paginate /api/contacts/:id/activity

Mutations/
  ContactMutations.swift    — create, update, delete with optimistic updates
  PropertyMutations.swift   — create, update, delete with optimistic updates
  StudioMutations.swift     — generate content via /api/studio/generate
```

**Data Layer Configuration:**

```swift
@Observable
final class ContactsRepository {
    private let apiClient: APIClient
    private let database: AppDatabase
    private let staleTime: TimeInterval = 5 * 60      // 5 minutes
    private let maxRetries: Int = 2

    private(set) var contacts: [Contact] = []
    private(set) var isLoading = false
    private(set) var error: Error?
    private var lastFetchedAt: Date?

    var isStale: Bool {
        guard let lastFetchedAt else { return true }
        return Date().timeIntervalSince(lastFetchedAt) > staleTime
    }

    func fetch(forceRefresh: Bool = false) async {
        guard forceRefresh || isStale else { return }

        // Return cached data immediately if available
        if contacts.isEmpty {
            contacts = (try? await database.fetchAllContacts()) ?? []
        }

        // Fetch from network
        isLoading = true
        defer { isLoading = false }

        do {
            let remote = try await apiClient.get("/api/contacts", as: [Contact].self)
            try await database.upsertContacts(remote)
            contacts = remote
            lastFetchedAt = Date()
            error = nil
        } catch {
            // If offline, GRDB data is already loaded above
            if contacts.isEmpty {
                self.error = error
            }
        }
    }
}
```

---

#### ADR-5: Navigation

**Decision:** SwiftUI `NavigationStack` + `TabView` with programmatic navigation

**Justification:**
- SwiftUI's built-in navigation (`NavigationStack`, `TabView`, `NavigationLink`, `.navigationDestination()`) is the first-party solution for iOS app navigation. It provides native transitions, gestures (swipe-back), and system integration (e.g., toolbar, search) without any third-party dependency
- `NavigationStack` (iOS 16+) supports type-safe, value-based navigation with `NavigationPath`, which enables programmatic navigation (push/pop from code) — essential for deep linking and notification routing
- The navigation hierarchy is simple enough that SwiftUI's declarative navigation is clearer and more maintainable than any third-party routing library. There is no React Navigation equivalent needed because SwiftUI's navigation IS the native navigation

**Navigator Hierarchy:**

```
SkyeApp (@main)
├── SplashView
├── AuthNavigationStack (NavigationStack)
│   ├── WelcomeView
│   └── SignInView
├── MainTabView (TabView)
│   ├── ChatTab (NavigationStack)
│   │   ├── ChatView
│   │   └── ConversationListView
│   ├── PeopleTab (NavigationStack)
│   │   ├── PeopleView
│   │   └── ContactDetailView
│   ├── PropertiesTab (NavigationStack)
│   │   ├── PropertiesView
│   │   └── PropertyDetailView
│   └── StudioTab (NavigationStack)
│       ├── StudioView
│       ├── StudioTemplateView
│       └── StudioResultView
├── CameraView (.fullScreenCover)
├── NotificationPreferencesView (.sheet)
└── SettingsView (.sheet)
```

**Deep Link Configuration:**

```swift
@main
struct SkyeApp: App {
    @State private var navigationState = NavigationState()

    var body: some Scene {
        WindowGroup {
            RootView()
                .environment(navigationState)
                .onOpenURL { url in
                    handleDeepLink(url)
                }
        }
    }

    private func handleDeepLink(_ url: URL) {
        guard let components = URLComponents(url: url, resolvingAgainstBaseURL: false) else { return }

        let pathComponents = components.path.split(separator: "/").map(String.init)

        switch pathComponents.first {
        case "chat":
            let conversationId = pathComponents.count > 1 ? pathComponents[1] : nil
            navigationState.navigate(to: .chat(conversationId: conversationId))
        case "contacts":
            guard let contactId = pathComponents.count > 1 ? pathComponents[1] : nil else { return }
            navigationState.navigate(to: .contactDetail(contactId: contactId))
        case "properties":
            guard let propertyId = pathComponents.count > 1 ? pathComponents[1] : nil else { return }
            navigationState.navigate(to: .propertyDetail(propertyId: propertyId))
        default:
            break
        }
    }
}

// URL schemes: skye:// (configured in Info.plist CFBundleURLTypes)
// Universal links: https://app.skye.com/... (configured via Associated Domains entitlement)
```

**Auth-Gated Deep Links:** When a deep link arrives and the user is not authenticated, the target route is stored in `NavigationState` (`pendingDeepLink`). After successful authentication, the navigation system checks for a pending deep link and navigates to it, then clears the stored value.

---

### 4. Risk Register

| ID | Risk | Likelihood (1-5) | Impact (1-5) | Score (LxI) | Mitigation | Contingency |
|----|------|:--:|:--:|:--:|------------|-------------|
| R-01 | **SSE streaming performance** — While native Swift eliminates the JS bridge overhead, rapid token rendering (20+ per second) in SwiftUI could still cause view diffing overhead if the view hierarchy is not structured carefully | 2 | 3 | **6** | Use `URLSession.AsyncBytes` for zero-overhead streaming. Batch UI updates: accumulate tokens in an `@Observable` property and debounce view updates to 50ms intervals using `Task.sleep`. Use `List` with stable `id` for each message to minimize diffing. Profile with Instruments early in Phase 2. | If streaming causes frame drops: use `CADisplayLink` to synchronize updates with the display refresh rate. Alternatively, render streaming text using `UITextView` via `UIViewRepresentable` (more efficient for rapid text appends than SwiftUI `Text`). |
| R-02 | **Google OAuth redirect URI conflicts with web app** — iOS app and web app may register conflicting redirect URIs in Google Cloud Console, causing one to break | 2 | 4 | **8** | Create a separate iOS OAuth client ID in Google Cloud Console (not reuse the web client ID). iOS client ID uses a reversed client ID scheme (`com.googleusercontent.apps.{ID}`), which cannot conflict with web redirect URIs. Document both client IDs clearly in `.xcconfig` files. | If conflict occurs: create an entirely new Google Cloud project for the mobile app. This is an organizational overhead, not a technical blocker. |
| R-03 | **Apple App Store rejection** — Privacy violations, HIG non-compliance, crashes, missing account deletion, private API usage by third-party SDKs | 4 | 5 | **20** | Dedicated Phase 7 audit (accessibility, privacy manifest, SDK audit, performance). Phase 8 has explicit compliance checklist with 10 items. Account deletion implemented as a requirement (task 7.13). Pre-submission review using Apple's common rejection checklist. Test on minimum 3 different iPhone models. | Budget 1-2 weeks for rejection response. First rejection is informational (they tell you exactly what to fix). Have a "rejection response" playbook: fix within 48 hours, re-submit with detailed notes. Native Swift apps generally have a smoother review process than cross-platform apps. |
| R-04 | **Offline sync conflicts** — User edits a contact on mobile while offline, same contact is edited on web. When mobile comes online, conflicts arise | 3 | 3 | **9** | Implement "last-write-wins" strategy with timestamps. Each entity has `updatedAt` field. On sync, compare mobile's `updatedAt` with server's `updatedAt`. If server is newer, server wins (mobile changes are lost). If mobile is newer, mobile wins. This is simple and predictable. | If users report data loss: implement field-level merge (compare individual fields, not entire records). If that is insufficient: implement conflict resolution UI ("Your version vs. server version — choose one"). This adds complexity and should only be built if users actually encounter this issue. |
| R-05 | **Push notification delivery reliability** — APNs is best-effort; notifications may be delayed, deduplicated, or dropped by iOS power management | 3 | 3 | **9** | Use direct APNs integration with receipt tracking. Implement notification receipt tracking: when backend sends push, log the push ID; when app receives push, confirm receipt via API; monitor delivery rate in analytics. Set notification priority to `apns-priority: 10` for time-sensitive notifications. | If delivery rate falls below 90%: investigate whether iOS is throttling (often due to too many silent pushes). Implement in-app notification polling as backup: on app foreground, fetch unread notifications from server. This ensures no notification is permanently lost. |
| R-06 | **Chat screen memory pressure with long conversations** — 500+ messages with markdown, tool cards, and images could exhaust memory on older devices (2-3 GB RAM) | 3 | 4 | **12** | Task 2.12 limits in-memory messages to 200. SwiftUI `List` provides automatic view recycling. Images use Nuke or `AsyncImage` with disk caching (not memory caching) for inline images. Measure memory with Xcode Instruments during Phase 2 on iPhone SE. | If memory still exceeds 300 MB: reduce in-memory limit to 100 messages. Implement message "windowing" where only the viewport +/- 50 messages are loaded. Move markdown parsing to a pre-computed step (parse on insert, store `AttributedString` data in GRDB) to reduce per-render computation. |
| R-07 | **Swift concurrency and actor isolation complexity** — Swift's strict concurrency model (Sendable checking, actor isolation, main actor requirements) can surface compile-time errors that are time-consuming to resolve, especially with older SDKs | 3 | 3 | **9** | Adopt Swift 6 strict concurrency checking from day one (`-strict-concurrency=complete` in build settings). Use `@MainActor` consistently for all UI-related types. Use `actor` for data isolation (API client, streaming service). Train team on structured concurrency patterns before Phase 1. | If concurrency issues block progress: temporarily use `@preconcurrency` or `nonisolated(unsafe)` annotations as escape hatches, with TODO comments to resolve properly. These do not affect runtime behavior but suppress compile-time checks. |
| R-08 | **Token refresh race conditions** — Multiple concurrent API calls all detect a 401; each independently tries to refresh the token; only the first succeeds, others fail | 4 | 3 | **12** | Task 1.9 implements an actor-based isolation pattern in the API client: the first 401 triggers a refresh; all subsequent requests during refresh are suspended using Swift's `AsyncStream` or `CheckedContinuation`; when refresh completes, all suspended requests are retried with the new token. Swift actors make this pattern naturally thread-safe. | If the actor pattern has edge cases: implement a simpler approach — proactively refresh the token 60 seconds before expiry (using the `exp` claim from the JWT, decoded with a lightweight JWT parser). This eliminates 401s entirely for normal usage. The 401 handler becomes a safety net, not the primary mechanism. |
| R-09 | **Image caching memory pressure on older devices** — Property images (10+ per listing, high resolution) could cause memory warnings and OOM kills | 3 | 4 | **12** | Use Nuke SPM (`kean/Nuke`) which provides automatic memory-aware caching with configurable memory and disk cache limits. Configure max memory cache to 100 MB and max disk cache to 500 MB. Load thumbnails in list views, full resolution only in detail view gallery. Use progressive JPEG loading with Nuke's `ImagePipeline` configuration. | If OOM kills occur: reduce memory cache to 50 MB. Implement explicit image recycling using `ImagePipeline.shared.cache.removeAll()` when navigating away from galleries. If still insufficient: serve WebP format from backend (40% smaller than JPEG at equivalent quality — iOS 14+ supports WebP natively). |
| R-10 | **App startup time exceeding 2s budget** — Database migrations, cache hydration, auth check, Sentry init, and navigation render may exceed 2 seconds total | 2 | 3 | **6** | Native Swift apps compile to optimized machine code and have inherently faster startup than interpreted/JIT runtimes. Defer non-critical initialization: Sentry can init async after first render. Database migrations run in background using `Task.detached`. `@AppStorage` reads are synchronous and <1ms. Auth check is a single UserDefaults read (not a network call). Launch screen covers the initialization period gracefully. | If startup exceeds 2s: profile with Xcode Instruments Time Profiler to identify the bottleneck. Common fixes: lazy-load tab views (only import data when first visited), reduce initial query prefetching, optimize GRDB migration checks. |
| R-11 | **Privacy Manifest completeness** — Apple rejects the app because a third-party SDK uses a Required Reason API that is not declared in the app's privacy manifest | 4 | 4 | **16** | Task 7.4 creates the privacy manifest. Task 7.6 audits every third-party SPM package for required reason API usage. Use Xcode's "Generate Privacy Report" feature (Product > Generate Privacy Report) to automatically detect missing declarations. Cross-reference with Apple's list of required reason APIs. | If rejected for privacy manifest: Apple's rejection message will specify exactly which API category is missing. Add the declaration, rebuild, and re-submit. Turnaround: 24-48 hours. This is one of the most fixable rejection reasons. |
| R-12 | **Third-party SDK introducing private API usage** — A dependency (or one of its transitive dependencies) calls a private Apple API, which Apple's automated scanner detects during review | 2 | 5 | **10** | Task 7.6 audits SDKs. Before submission: run `nm` and `otool` on the built binary to scan for private API symbols. Use third-party tools like App Store Compliance Scanner. Prefer Apple frameworks over third-party SPM packages where possible. Pin all SPM dependency versions to exact versions using `.exact("x.y.z")` in `Package.swift`. | If rejected: the rejection message names the specific API. Identify which dependency uses it. Either update the dependency (if the maintainer has fixed it), replace it with an alternative, or fork and patch. Typically resolvable within 1-3 days. |

**Risk Heat Map:**

```
Impact  5│    R-03
        4│ R-02  R-11  R-06,R-08,R-09   R-12
        3│ R-10  R-07  R-04,R-05   R-01
        2│
        1│
         └─────────────────────────────────
           1     2     3     4     5
                    Likelihood
```

**Top 3 Risks by Score:**
1. **R-03** (App Store Rejection) — Score 20 — Mitigated by dedicated Phase 7 + Phase 8 compliance process
2. **R-11** (Privacy Manifest) — Score 16 — Mitigated by automated detection tools + explicit audit task
3. **R-06, R-08, R-09** (tied at Score 12) — Performance/resource risks, mitigated by early measurement and conservative defaults

**Note on Reduced Risk vs. React Native:** Several risks from the React Native version are eliminated or significantly reduced in the native Swift implementation:
- **R-01** (SSE streaming performance) drops from Score 12 to Score 6 — no JS bridge overhead
- **R-07** (Hermes engine compatibility) is replaced by R-07 (Swift concurrency complexity) at Score 9 — a development velocity risk rather than a runtime crash risk
- **R-10** (App startup time) drops from Score 9 to Score 6 — native binary loads faster than JS bundle
- React Native-specific risks (Hermes polyfills, Metro bundler issues, native module linking) are completely eliminated

---

### 5. Backend Changes Required

Despite the principle that "the backend is largely unchanged," the following changes are required. These are minimal, targeted modifications — not a backend rewrite.

| Priority | Endpoint | Change Type | Phase Needed | Effort Est. | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|:---:|:-----------:|-------------|
| **P0** | `GET /api/health` | New | 0 | 1h | Returns `{ "status": "ok", "timestamp": "..." }`. No auth required. Used by native app to verify connectivity before auth flow. Also useful for uptime monitoring. |
| **P0** | `POST /api/auth/native` | New | 0 | 8h | Accepts `{ code, codeVerifier, redirectUri }` from native Google PKCE flow via `ASWebAuthenticationSession`. Server-side: exchanges code with Google (using client secret stored server-side), obtains Google tokens, creates or updates Supabase user via `supabase.auth.admin.createUser()` or `signInWithIdToken()`, returns `{ accessToken, refreshToken, user }`. Must handle: new user creation, existing user login, invalid code, expired code. |
| **P0** | `DELETE /api/user/delete` | New | 0 | 6h | Apple App Store requirement. Accepts authenticated request. Cascades deletion: user data, contacts, properties, conversations, messages, push subscriptions, studio drafts. Returns confirmation. Must be idempotent. Should send confirmation email before deletion (with 24h grace period if possible). |
| **P1** | `POST /api/push/subscribe` | Modify | 5 | 4h | Currently accepts web push subscription object. Modify to accept: `{ token, platform: "ios" | "web", tokenType: "apns" | "web-push" }`. Store platform discriminator in push_subscriptions table. Deduplicate by (user_id, device_token) pair. Remove stale tokens (not seen in 30 days). |
| **P1** | `POST /api/push/send` | Modify | 5 | 4h | Currently sends via Web Push. Add provider routing: if `platform === "ios"`, send via direct APNs (using .p8 key and HTTP/2 APNs API). If `platform === "web"`, send via existing Web Push. Abstract into a `PushProvider` interface. |
| **P2** | All 129 routes | Audit | 0 | 4h | (1) Verify every error response is JSON, not HTML (e.g., Next.js default error pages return HTML). Add global error handler that wraps all responses in `{ error: { code, message } }` format. (2) Verify no route checks `Origin` or `Referer` header (native apps don't send these). (3) Verify `Content-Type: application/json` header is set on all responses. |

**Total Backend Effort:** ~27 hours (~3.5 developer-days)

**CORS Note:** Native iOS apps make HTTP requests via `URLSession` (Apple's native networking stack), not a browser. This means there is no CORS preflight — the `Origin` header is not sent. If any backend middleware rejects requests without an `Origin` header, it will break the native app. The audit must verify that CORS middleware either (a) allows requests without `Origin` or (b) is bypassed for API routes.

---

### 6. Technology Stack — Final Bill of Materials

#### Core Framework

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| Swift | 6.0+ | Programming language | Apache-2.0 | Built into Xcode |
| SwiftUI | iOS 16+ | Declarative UI framework | Proprietary (Apple) | Built into iOS SDK |
| Xcode | 16+ | IDE, compiler, build system | Proprietary (Apple) | Required for development |
| iOS Deployment Target | 16.0+ | Minimum supported iOS version | N/A | Covers ~95% of active devices |

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| `NavigationStack` | iOS 16+ | Type-safe stack navigation | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `TabView` | iOS 13+ | Bottom tab navigator | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `NavigationPath` | iOS 16+ | Programmatic navigation state | Built-in | Enables deep linking |

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| `@Observable` macro | iOS 17+ / backport | Fine-grained observation for client state | Built-in | Observation framework |
| `@AppStorage` | iOS 14+ | Reactive UserDefaults access for simple persistence | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `UserDefaults` | iOS 2+ | Key-value persistence (theme, flags, sync timestamps) | Built-in | Foundation framework |

| Keychain Services | iOS 2+ | Secure storage for refresh tokens | Built-in | Security framework |

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| `URLSession` | iOS 7+ | HTTP networking, SSE streaming via `AsyncBytes` | Built-in | Foundation framework |
| `NWPathMonitor` | iOS 12+ | Network connectivity detection | Built-in | Network framework |

| Package | Version | Purpose | License | Privacy Manifest |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|:----------------:|

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|

| SwiftUI Gestures | iOS 13+ | Gesture recognition (`.gesture()`, `DragGesture`, `MagnifyGesture`) | Built-in | Replaces Gesture Handler |
| `AsyncImage` | iOS 15+ | Basic async image loading | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator` | iOS 10+ | Haptic feedback (impact, notification, selection) | Built-in | UIKit |
| `.sheet()` / `.presentationDetents()` | iOS 16+ | Bottom sheet modal component | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `List` | iOS 13+ | Virtualized list with recycling | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |
| `.searchable()` | iOS 15+ | Integrated search bar | Built-in | Part of SwiftUI |

| Component | Version | Purpose | License | Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------|

| `Swift Testing` | Swift 6.0+ | Modern test framework with `@Test` macro | Built-in | Ships with Xcode 16+ |
| `XCUITest` | Xcode 16+ | UI automation testing | Built-in | Ships with Xcode |
| Maestro | latest | E2E testing framework (YAML-based flows) | Apache-2.0 | CLI tool, optional alternative to XCUITest |

#### Build & Deployment

| Tool | Purpose | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| `xcodebuild` | Command-line builds, archiving, testing | Ships with Xcode |
| Xcode Cloud or GitHub Actions | CI/CD pipeline | Automated builds, tests, deployment |
| fastlane (optional) | Build automation, screenshots, deployment | Ruby gem, optional convenience layer |
| `xcrun altool` / Xcode Organizer | App Store / TestFlight upload | Ships with Xcode |
| App Store Connect API | Programmatic submission and management | REST API |

**Total third-party SPM dependencies:** ~5 packages
**Estimated IPA size:** 15-25 MB (native Swift compiles smaller than JS bundles; no Hermes engine, no Metro bundle)

**Version Pinning Policy:** All SPM dependencies are pinned to exact minor versions using `.upToNextMinor(from: "x.y.0")` in `Package.swift`. This prevents unexpected breaking changes. Major version upgrades require explicit team decision and testing.

---

### 7. Execution Principles

These are not aspirational guidelines. They are contractual obligations that every developer on the project agrees to follow.

**7.1 — One Phase at a Time**
Never start the next phase until the current phase passes every item on its verification checklist. "Mostly done" is not done. If a phase is blocked, the blockage is resolved — it is not worked around by jumping ahead. The only exception is explicit parallelization noted in the dependency graph (Section 2), and only with separate developers on separate branches.

**7.2 — Four States From Day One**
Every screen renders four states: loading (skeleton), success (data), empty (no data + CTA), and error (message + retry). These are not added in Phase 7. They are built as the screen is built, enforced by the `ScreenContainer` view from task 1.16. A PR that shows a screen with only a success state will be rejected.

**7.3 — Dark Mode Is Not a Phase 7 Task**
Every component is built with both light and dark variants from the moment it is created. The theming system from task 1.3 makes this automatic if developers use `Color` assets in the asset catalog or theme tokens via the `ThemeManager` instead of hardcoded colors. Phase 7's dark mode audit is a verification step, not a creation step. If the audit finds 50 issues, something went wrong in Phases 2-6.

**7.4 — Accessibility Labels Are Built In, Not Bolted On**
Every interactive element receives an `.accessibilityLabel()` and appropriate `.accessibilityAddTraits()` when it is created. Every image receives a descriptive label. This is enforced by code review — a PR without accessibility labels on new interactive elements will be rejected. Phase 7's accessibility audit verifies correctness and completeness, but the labels themselves are already there. SwiftUI provides excellent built-in accessibility — use it.

**7.5 — Performance Is Measured Continuously**
After Phase 1, every phase includes a performance check: cold start time, list scroll FPS, screen transition time, and memory usage. These are recorded in a shared spreadsheet or tracking document. If any metric regresses by more than 10% from the previous phase, the regression is investigated and fixed before the phase is closed. Performance is not "optimized at the end" — it is maintained throughout. Use Xcode Instruments (Time Profiler, Core Animation, Allocations) for measurement.

**7.6 — Every PR Is Reviewed Against the Spec**
The reviewer's first action is to find the relevant task in this document and verify that the PR satisfies the task's requirements. PRs that add unrequested features, skip error handling, omit tests, or deviate from the ADRs are rejected. The spec is the source of truth, not the developer's interpretation.

**7.7 — Demo at Every Phase Gate**
At the end of each phase, the app is demoed to stakeholders (product owner, designer, or other team members) on a physical device. Feedback is collected in writing. Critical feedback (bugs, UX failures) is resolved before the phase is marked complete. Non-critical feedback (preferences, nice-to-haves) is logged for consideration but does not block the phase.

**7.8 — No Undocumented Dependencies**
Every SPM package added to the project must appear in the Bill of Materials (Section 6) or be explicitly justified in the PR that adds it. Random packages discovered via Stack Overflow are not added without team discussion. This prevents dependency bloat, license conflicts, and privacy manifest gaps. Prefer Apple frameworks over third-party packages wherever possible.

**7.9 — Offline Is Not an Afterthought**
Every screen that displays server data must work offline from the moment it is built. This means: data is written to GRDB during the fetch cycle, queries fall back to GRDB when offline, and an offline indicator is visible to the user. This is part of the screen's definition of done, not a future enhancement.

**7.10 — Test Coverage Is a Phase Exit Criterion**
Each phase's task list includes unit test and E2E test tasks. The phase cannot exit until these tests pass. The minimum bar: every API interaction has a unit test (XCTest or Swift Testing), every critical user flow has a Maestro or XCUITest E2E test, and code coverage on new code is above 70% (measured per phase, not globally). Run `xcodebuild test` in CI for every PR.

---

*This document governs the entire Skye iOS build. Changes require a formal change request reviewed by the project lead, with impact analysis on the dependency graph, risk register, and timeline.*
